---
title: "1.1 The Importance of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    auto-slide: 9000
    auto-slide-stoppable: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
execute:
  echo: true
---

## A backtest is useful only when it rehearses the live strategy.

::: {.split-visual-slide}
::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
| Mode | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Backtest | historical feed | simulated orders |
| Live | market feed | executable orders |
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/live-strategy-rehearsal.svg){fig-alt="Flow from same strategy logic to same timing rules to same execution assumptions"}
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
A backtest is useful only when it rehearses the live strategy. Historical data
and live data are different feeds, but the trading rule should be the same rule.
That means the timing rule, the order type, the venue, and the price field have
to be explicit. If the backtest uses assumptions that the live system cannot
use, the performance estimate belongs to a strategy we are not actually going
to trade.
:::

## Published performance still hides implementation choices.

| Published phrase | Execution decision that changes returns |
|---|---|
| "buy at the open" | MOO auction or market order after the open |
| "use the close" | primary auction, consolidated last, or settlement |
| "trigger on price" | bid, ask, last trade, high, or low |
| "trade S&P futures" | stock close at 4:00 or futures close at 4:15 |

::: {.notes}
Published strategies often report the idea and the historical result, but they
cannot describe every execution choice without distracting from the main point.
Those omitted choices matter. Buying at the open can mean an auction order or a
market order after the open. Using the close can mean a primary exchange auction
price, a consolidated last trade, or a futures settlement. Independent
backtesting forces these choices into the strategy definition.
:::

## Independent backtesting turns an idea into an executable rule.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/executable-rule-pipeline.svg){fig-alt="Pipeline from published idea to executable rule through instrument, venue, timestamp, order type, backtest code, and execution code"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Independent backtesting is not only a check on someone else's return number. It
is the process that turns an informal strategy idea into an executable rule. We
start with the published idea, then specify the instrument, venue, timestamp,
and order type. Once those decisions are encoded in the backtest, the same
logic can be carried toward automated execution. That continuity is what makes
the backtest operational rather than decorative.
:::

## Pitfall checks must happen before the equity curve earns trust.

| Strategy setting | Hidden feasibility check |
|---|---|
| Long-short stock book | Were the short names borrowable? |
| Intermarket futures pair | Did both market closes occur together? |
| Published rule after release | Did post-publication data still work? |

::: {.notes}
After a strategy is implemented, the first question is not whether the equity
curve looks smooth. The first question is whether the test describes feasible
trades. For a long-short stock book, the short side may depend on borrow
availability. For an intermarket futures pair, two closing prices may not be
observed at the same time. These checks belong before interpretation because
most pitfalls make backtested performance look too good.
:::

## True out-of-sample testing begins after the rule is frozen.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/out-of-sample-boundary.svg){fig-alt="Timeline showing research period, publication boundary, and validation period"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The phrase out-of-sample can be misleading before the rule is frozen. If a
researcher sees weak validation results and then changes the parameters, that
validation period has become part of the research process. Publication creates
a cleaner boundary because the rule is now cast in stone. Data arriving after
that boundary can test the published rule without the same hidden opportunity
for revision.
:::

## Backtesting is an experiment cycle with a hard validation boundary.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/backtesting-experiment-cycle.svg){fig-alt="Experiment cycle from hypothesis to implementation to backtest result, revision, frozen rule, and walk-forward test"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Backtesting follows the scientific method. We start with a hypothesis about an
arbitrage opportunity or market inefficiency. We implement that hypothesis,
test it, and revise it when the evidence is not good enough. That loop is
valuable, but it is not unlimited. Once the rule is frozen, the next test is a
walk-forward or post-publication test, not another chance to keep tuning the
same validation data.
:::

## A credible backtest can answer three live-trading questions.

| Question | Evidence in the backtest |
|---|---|
| Can the same rule run live? | one strategy function, two data feeds |
| Was the information available? | signals delayed to decision time |
| Was the market access realistic? | venue, order, borrow, and clock checks |

::: {.notes}
Before trusting a backtest, reduce the review to three live-trading questions.
First, can the same strategy rule run live if we replace historical data with a
live feed? Second, was every signal based on information available at the
decision time? Third, did the backtest use market access that the live trader
could actually obtain? If the answer to any of these questions is no, the result
is still research, not execution evidence.
:::
